---
title: "Chicago Crime Arrest Prediction"
format:
  html:
    toc: false
---

# Project Overview

This project studies whether a reported crime incident in Chicago results in an arrest. Using the City of Chicago's public crime records, the analysis combines structured data cleaning, exploratory analysis, and out-of-time predictive modeling to understand how offense type, incident setting, time, and geography shape arrest outcomes.

The website is organized around the three core notebooks in the project:

- [Data Cleaning](Data_cleaning.html): combines yearly crime files, handles missing values, standardizes field types, and creates the cleaned modeling and mapping datasets.
- [EDA](EDA.html): summarizes temporal, categorical, and spatial patterns in crime volume and arrest rates, and translates those patterns into modeling expectations.
- [Modeling](Modeling.html): compares baseline and ensemble classifiers using out-of-time validation, feature ablation, and SHAP-based interpretation for the strongest final model.

## Data and Study Window

- Source: City of Chicago Data Portal, `Crimes - 2001 to Present`
- Analysis window: 2016-2025 complete-year records
- Final task: binary classification of `Arrest`
- Main evaluation metric: `PR-AUC`

## Main Findings

- Arrest prediction is a clearly imbalanced classification problem, so precision-recall performance is more informative than accuracy alone.
- The strongest models are boosting methods, with LightGBM currently achieving the best out-of-time test `PR-AUC`.
- Feature ablation shows that time variables alone are not enough. Crime type, location context, and community-level geography provide the largest gains.
- The SHAP analysis for the final best model reinforces the same substantive story: offense category, situational context, and spatial structure are the main drivers of predicted arrest probability.

## Notebook Guide

The notebooks are presented as rendered project pages rather than rewritten summaries, so each page preserves the original figures, result tables, and narrative interpretation. Code is foldable to keep the reading flow focused on the analytical story while still leaving the implementation visible.
